title: OpenNeko plugin marketplace
description: Single-file catalog of plugins, modeled on Claude Code's marketplace.json plus the security fields the OpenNeko CLI enforces (integrity, permissions, capabilities). Publish your own marketplace.json at a stable URL and operators can trust it with `openneko marketplace add <url>`.

name.description: Human-readable name. Shown when the operator adds this marketplace.

owner.description: GitHub org / individual / company that publishes this marketplace.

integrity.description: Pinned npm dist.integrity hash. Use the all-`a` placeholder pattern ONLY in combination with `draft: true` while the package is still pre-publish.

draft.description: Set to true when the version isn't on npm yet (chicken-and-egg case for a brand-new package whose first publish hasn't fired). `validate-live` skips every live check for draft entries — they're treated as informational. Flip to false and pin the real integrity hash once the publish workflow has succeeded.

provenanceWaived.description: Set to true for legacy versions that were published before the publish workflow started using --provenance. `validate-live` still enforces integrity but skips the provenance check. Don't use on new versions — bump and re-publish with --provenance instead.

permissions.description: What the plugin needs from the runtime: outbound network hosts and operator-supplied env vars. Same shape lives in the installed manifest entry.

network.description: Hostnames the sandbox is allowed to reach. Enforced at the VM boundary.

env.description: Env vars the operator must supply. `openneko install` prompts for required ones and refuses to complete if missing.

key.description: UPPER_SNAKE_CASE env var name the plugin reads.

secret.description: Hide the value at prompt; the CLI will not echo it back.

inject.description: How a secret reaches the plugin VM. 'egress': held gateway-side and substituted by the proxy onto outbound requests — the box sees only a placeholder (for credentials sent to an external API, e.g. a bot token). 'box' (default): the value lives in the VM, for secrets compared locally and never sent out (e.g. a webhook signing secret).

capabilities.description: Surfaces this plugin contributes. The keyset declares what the plugin does — `action`, `auth`, and/or `connect`. At least one must be present.

action.description: The plugin contributes one or more named action handlers.

auth.description: The plugin acts as the SSO provider. Singleton — only one installed plugin may declare this.

providerLabel.description: Short label rendered on the sign-in button (e.g. "Scalekit", "Okta").

connect.description: The plugin offers a per-operator OAuth connector. Non-singleton — any number of installed plugins may declare this, and each operator connects independently. Credentials land in the per-operator section of the secrets store.

providerLabel.description: Display label shown on the Connect button + status row (e.g. "Google Workspace", "GitHub").

scopes.description: OAuth scopes the connector will request at consent time.

flow.description: Flow type the worker should drive. v1 supports oauth2-pkce only.

channel.description: The plugin is a frontend (channel) — Slack, Telegram, voice, etc. Non-singleton; the worker projects modality-free InteractionEvents into the substrate inside the plugin VM and normalizes inbound payloads back into agent intents.

ingress.description: How inbound arrives: an HTTP webhook, a persistent socket, or none (outbound-only).

capabilityProfile.description: What the substrate can carry — the only thing a projection may branch on. Additive: new fields default such that existing channels are unaffected, so unknown properties are tolerated.

description.description: Short description the agent uses to pick this kind.

default_mode.description: Seeded approval policy when the plugin is freshly installed. auto = run inline with no human gate (read-only kinds). ask = suspend agent turn, render approval card to the user (externally observable kinds). deny = never invokable without explicit opt-in. Operators can override later in /settings/rules. Defaults to ask when omitted — safer to surprise the user with a prompt than a side effect. Accepts either a scalar (applies to all scopes) or a per-scope object {external?, internal?} when the kind needs different defaults depending on the scope it's invoked under.
